query: treatment of choice for childhood thyrotoxicosis

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Radioiodine Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Children with thyrotoxicosis may be treated with methimazole, radioiodine therapy, or thyroidectomy. Methimazole is the first-line therapy for Graves disease in children, typically administered for 1 to 2 years, as some children may achieve remission. Radioiodine therapy is not recommended for children aged 5 or younger. Additionally, PTU should be avoided in children due to its risk of hepatotoxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Graves Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: All three options have pros and cons, and there is no consensus on which one is the best option. It is very important to discuss all three options in detail with the patients and make an individualized decision.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Radioiodine Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy: Radioiodine therapy, as mentioned above, is contraindicated during pregnancy; however, antithyroid drugs may be used, and thyroidectomy may be considered for severe hyperthyroidism. [47] During pregnancy, thionamide drugs should be administered in a titrated dose regimen. PTU is preferred during the first trimester of pregnancy due to the teratogenic risks associated with methimazole, such as aplasia cutis and choanal or esophageal atresia. Maternal TSH receptor antibodies can be measured to help assess the risk of fetal hyperthyroidism. [2] [52] [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Radioiodine Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Notably, radiation therapy is contraindicated during pregnancy and lactation, and conception should be avoided for 6 to 12 months for both males and females. A small risk of thyrotoxicosis exacerbation exists in the first month after treatment due to the release of preformed thyroid hormones. Additionally, radioiodine therapy is a definitive risk factor for developing Graves orbitopathy. [23] [41] [52] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Radioactive Iodine Therapy, " for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyrotoxicosis -- Treatment / Management -- Thionamide Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Methimazole is administered at a dosage of 15 to 30 mg/d for 4 to 8 weeks in the treatment of Graves disease, during which most patients achieve a euthyroid state. Once euthyroidism is established, treatment can proceed with 1 of 2 approaches. In the block-replace method, the same dose of thionamide is continued to inhibit thyroid hormone production, while levothyroxine is added to maintain euthyroidism. Alternatively, the thionamide dose can be gradually reduced to allow endogenous thyroid hormone synthesis, thereby sustaining a euthyroid state. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Methimazole ," for more information. In Graves disease, long-term remission is achieved in about 50% of patients treated with thionamide drugs. However, a drawback of thionamides is the uncertainty of relapse after discontinuation. Prolonged treatment beyond 18 months has not been shown to improve remission rates. Methimazole is more effective and has a longer half-life than PTU, allowing for once-daily dosing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is recognized as an appropriate descriptor in the clinical management of تمزق, thyroid disorders, بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, and ورم عضلي أملس, indicating that it may be used when documenting or planning treatment for each of these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS include dextrothyroxin as a specific child preparation, indicating that dextrothyroxin is a type of medication within the broader category of thyroid and antithyroid agents used to influence thyroid hormone activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid extracts and thyroid inhibitors represent a therapeutic class of agents that affect thyroid function, and within this class the specific subgroup known as thyreostatika is included. Thyreostatika are medications that act to modulate or suppress thyroid activity, and they are classified as a child category of the broader group of thyroid extracts and thyroid inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1443
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are a class of medications that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis, and they encompass specific agents such as diiodotyrosine and carbimazole. Both diiodotyrosine and carbimazole function as antithyroid drugs, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of reducing excessive thyroid hormone production in conditions like hyperthyroidism. Recognizing that these compounds belong to the broader category of antithyroid preparations helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options and understand their pharmacologic relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: a child of the Radioisotope study of the endocrine system, integrating nuclear medicine into thyroid cancer evaluation. Together, these entities and relationships form a comprehensive network linking respiratory disease classification, mediastinal anatomy, thoracic imaging techniques, and interventional procedures essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and multidisciplinary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 380
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS is positioned within the broader class of antithyroid preparations, indicating that it represents a subcategory of drugs that modulate thyroid activity. Within THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS, dextrothyroxin is identified as a specific child agent, reflecting its role as a distinct medication in this therapeutic group. Antithyroid preparations themselves encompass individual compounds such as dijodtyrosin and karbimazol, both of which are recognized as specific types of antithyroid agents. This hierarchical organization highlights how antithyroid preparations serve as the overarching category for various thyroid‑targeting drugs, with THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS and its child dextrothyroxin representing more specialized subsets, and dijodtyrosin and karbimazol exemplifying concrete pharmacologic options used in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 500
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS is a therapeutic group that encompasses dextrothyroxine as one of its child agents, while it is itself classified under the broader parent category of antithyroid preparations. Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents used to manage conditions of excess thyroid hormone production (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ, i.e., hyperthyroidism). These preparations are chemically related to diiodotyrosine, indicating that they belong to the diiodotyrosine class of compounds, and they also include specific drugs such as carbimazole (karbimazol). Together, these relationships illustrate how dextrothyroxine, antithyroid preparations, diiodotyrosine, and carbimazole are interconnected within the management of thyroid disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2201
retrieved_docs.content: Karbimazol, also known as carbimazole, is classified as an antithyroid preparation and is used in the treatment of hyperthyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified as a type of diiodotyrosine, a compound that participates in thyroid hormone synthesis, and they are employed to inhibit thyroid hormone production in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: agents in the subcategory “Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors.” This subcategory contains the child entity thyreostatika (thyroid‑suppressing agents) used in hyperthyroidism. The thyroid gland is the anatomical source and site of action for these hormones and for thyroid‑blocking agents, and it is identified as the primary finding site for hypothyroidism (Arabic: قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة). Together, these interconnected classifications provide clinicians, pharmacists, and health‑information systems with a structured framework to locate and relate drugs, anatomical terms, and disease entities, supporting accurate prescribing, comprehensive patient care, and effective disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: Chest imaging for metastatic carcinoma of thyroid is classified as a child procedure within the broader category of radioisotope studies of the endocrine system, indicating that this specific imaging approach is a sub‑type of endocrine radioisotope evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified within the broader category of medications and vaccines, reflecting their role as therapeutic agents used to manage thyroid disorders. These preparations encompass specific compounds such as karbimazol and dijodtyrosin, which are individual antithyroid drugs that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis. By being part of the medication and vaccine group, antithyroid preparations are recognized for their clinical importance in treating conditions like hyperthyroidism, and their inclusion of agents such as karbimazol and dijodtyrosin highlights the diversity of pharmacologic options available within this therapeutic class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1833
retrieved_docs.content: THYROID AND ANTITHYROID PREPARATIONS is a broad drug category that includes dextrothyroxin as one of its child preparations. Within the dextrothyroxin hierarchy, D [Preparations] is classified as a sub‑category, and D [Preparations] in turn serves as the parent of the synthetic antidiuretic agent desmopresin. Both desmopresin and the trometamol salt of ketorolac (trometamolová sůl ketorolaku) are contraindicated in patients with renal disorder (اضطراب الكلية). Recognizing this hierarchical relationship and the shared contraindication for kidney dysfunction is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.
